And it’s your problem if you have different opinion :)BTR. I stand for Rx :)

So you work with people, who, independent of their age, still lack the maturity, to be considered senior.

commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity

commenters.name: Len Jaffe
commenters.headline: Non-fungible, Pragmatic, Wizened - Cloud Whisperer / Host of Code Jam Columbus / Perl Monger / IaC DevOps Platform Engineer at Labcorp
commenters.commentText: The difference between developer's idea of "can be changed in the future"  and business' idea of it are that the developer values it more than the business values it. In fact, the business typically only values "solves the problem now". Maintainability is clawed back one enhancement crisis at a time. Look at how long it took the US auto industry to adopt quality as a real metric alongside quantity.  We bought Japanese cars despite heavy tariffs, because they'd last as long as three cars from the

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Len Jaffe you might be right, but businesses that focus on “now” only dies quickly. Or keep struggling over times. Without balance between now and future everyone is unhappy :(

commenters.name: Len Jaffe
commenters.headline: Non-fungible, Pragmatic, Wizened - Cloud Whisperer / Host of Code Jam Columbus / Perl Monger / IaC DevOps Platform Engineer at Labcorp
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov The devs know this. The management is only paid to think about this quarter. Also, debt is a perfectly acceptable finance tool to the wealthy. Only the turning class really worries about debt. That is why business management doesn't stress when we call it debt. We must start calling it risk, so that they with start to hear us.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Len Jaffe good catch.

commenters.name: Oleksandr Don
commenters.headline: Software Engineer at UKRSIBBANK BNP Paribas Group
commenters.commentText: IMO "Can be changed in the future" leads to accumulating "Architectural debt", rarely addressed in the future until major issue emerges. Chicken or egg situation, has little impact on business unless risk can be estimated and proven quantitatively.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: unfortunately, I’ve seen several businesses that couldn’t overcome technical debt during the scaling phase—especially performance-related debt—and they eventually died. Businesses need to stay focused on the future; that’s another side of this problem.Engineers often don’t understand what matters most to the business; sometimes even the business doesn’t know what matters most to it. And moreover, the business doesn’t understand that SOLID and DRY are designed not for fun, but to make systems mai

commenters.name: Oleksandr Don
commenters.headline: Software Engineer at UKRSIBBANK BNP Paribas Group
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov thank you for term correction.  I was not thinking in terms of SOLID. Brutal case of DB schema misdesign ignoring future read use cases led to few months debugging in prod freezing system with little clue(memory dumps, event logs, performance profiling). Third party DB exhausted the OS resources due to ineffective write ops lead to DB client failure throughout the developing product. Besides violating advertised response times for safety critical modules."Diffusion of responsib

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Oleksandr Don the same, SOLID, DRY, DB normal forms, microservises, hexagonal architecture, Clean Code. All these are required to support possibilities to make changes in the future. When business ignores future everyone is suffering :)

commenters.name: Oleksandr Don
commenters.headline: Software Engineer at UKRSIBBANK BNP Paribas Group
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov I will read through the suggested methodology. Thanks. Adapter (DB gateway) was in place so the problem was more or less contained.

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.headline: IT Consultant & Developer | Frontend-Focused | UX/UI-Minded | Fintech & Insurance Domain Experience
commenters.commentText: But the time for change can be from 15 minutes up to months in case code duplicates are used everywhere etc..

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Maryna Tochkova if ai could deal with those duplications, than who care? Disclaimer: ai isn’t capable of that yet.

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.headline: IT Consultant & Developer | Frontend-Focused | UX/UI-Minded | Fintech & Insurance Domain Experience
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov Code can be good and bad. Good code means same coding style, same principles, naming conventions etc. In case all of it is consistent - me, you and probably AI will understand it fast, change or scale it. If it’s a different file/component structure in each case, mixed naming conventions,  code duplication with slightly changed content with no logic changes - well … good luck for all of us including super talented AI 😅 But it’s only my point of view of course ☺️

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Maryna Tochkova have you ever tried to read low level JavaScript produced by babel? Have seen how many duplications are there?

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.headline: IT Consultant & Developer | Frontend-Focused | UX/UI-Minded | Fintech & Insurance Domain Experience
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov no I didn’t. Why would I do it, with wich purpose? Since it’s a compiler it has its own reasons to do so due to the way it works. We don’t maintain/change this code directly. Anyway, I worked with old legacy code a lot - the code was bad, buggy, it’s maintenance was incredibly painful. Some scripts were running from the places they shouldn’t - a nightmare. So in my experience code quality means a world ☺️

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Maryna Tochkova right, the question is what do you mean by code quality? How do you distinguish bad code from good code?

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.headline: IT Consultant & Developer | Frontend-Focused | UX/UI-Minded | Fintech & Insurance Domain Experience
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov good one is: me as a new team member can quickly understand the structure of the project and code itself, it is consistent and clean. I get the naming, the logic of how things are built. I understand where to change the code and how it will impact the rest - no surprises for me. I am sure, that the part of the code I have changed is a single state of truth - so if I want the error handler to behave differently I change it in one place and it affects all the others. I change the

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Maryna Tochkova doesn’t it mean that for you code has enough quality when it’s easy to you to make changes? Does code have enough quality when page loads 15 seconds? What other traits of quality could be?

commenters.name: Maryna Tochkova
commenters.headline: IT Consultant & Developer | Frontend-Focused | UX/UI-Minded | Fintech & Insurance Domain Experience
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov it means a lot things that’s my point. And it means that “𝗱𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝘄𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗼𝗿 𝗯𝗮𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀” is not really the case… At least for now. I vibe code - I refactor very long after. I do AI powered development with my experience in it - I get better quality. I guess at least for now AI can better understand and change good code. Same applies to us humans. But we probably talk about different things or h

commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity

Because AI can’t make any changes not introducing mistakes.From human perspective AI should create code to be understood by humans. From business perspective ai should create

commenters.name: Oleksandr Koreniak
commenters.headline: Senior C++ developer
commenters.commentText: AI-generated code is simply working code that works and is worth as much as the AI ​​model that generated it. It's simply another code producer in the code supply chain. An experienced engineer knows where and how to use it.

commenters.name: Yurii Kinakh
commenters.headline: CTO & Co-Founder at @Inspirit | Building payment infrastructure & systems that handle scale | Writing about where engineering actually gets stuck
commenters.commentText: Been thinking this way for a while. Once you stop treating code like art and start treating it like a tool that has to work and stay flexible, most quality debates dissolve.What's tricky tho is the cultural side. Some senior devs feel like their craft is being devaluated when you adopt this mindset. That tension is real and worth managing.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity

commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity

commenters.name: Sanyam Arya
commenters.headline: Leading and Shaping Technology and Teams
commenters.commentText: It shouldn't just run, 𝗶𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. Design principles are exactly what make that "𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗿" requirement actually possible.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Sanyam Arya didn’t I stated that?

commenters.name: Yitzhak Kornbluth
commenters.headline: Staff Software Engineer, looking for new opportunities
commenters.commentText: Yitzhak Kornbluth As for AI-written code that can only be maintained by AI: Even assuming that the AI can in fact reliably maintain it (something that quite a few engineers say they've seen not happening), that locks you in to AI, which could be a problem if the venture capital dries up and the AI companies need to raise their prices to compensate.

commenters.name: Mukesh A.
commenters.headline: Principal Software Engineer, Inventor, National Award Winner, .Net stack (C#), ASP.NETCore, Azure DevOps.
commenters.commentText: Spot on! I’ve seen people doing refactoring to use new tools just because they are hyped to solve the problem better and faster. Conveniently ignoring man hours to get it done and failing to analyse what these changes will add to overall business value and propositions- the things really matter.

commenters.name: Andre Prellwitz
commenters.headline: Full-stack .NET Developer
commenters.commentText: Software has this quality where it seems to work for the moment, but fails catastrophically without warning, given the right conditions.  That failure can include function, performance, security, or even legal.  Does this actually work, is it fast enough to compete, is it compatible with our security posture, can we afford licensing?  It's a really low bar to simply argue efficacy.

commenters.name: Peter Ovchynnikov
commenters.headline: Building software with AI since 2018 ▪️ Co-CEO at Speed & Function ▪️Entrepreneur ▪️ Classical engineering × AI creativity
commenters.commentText: Let's consider software that fails without warning as not working.  E. g. work for the moment, but fails catastrophically without warning = NOT WORKING CODE. Code that has security issue = NOT WORKING CODE. If page load 15 seconds = NOT WORKING CODE. I don't see contradiction here.

commenters.name: Andre Prellwitz
commenters.headline: Full-stack .NET Developer
commenters.commentText: Peter Ovchynnikov Maybe it's a language or culture effect, but saying something "works" means something entirely different than what you postulated.Of course if AI code passes security scans, meets intuitive performance goals, considers licensing, handles edge cases, etc., there's no issue with it.  But I suspect that--especially for vibe coding--most AI code stops at the conventional definition of "working" and is not prompted, budgeted, or even capable of fully handling these aspects (yet).To

commenters.name: Iharob Al Asimi
commenters.headline: Senior Software Engineer
commenters.commentText: AI code can certainly be modified later by AI. But i increasingly becomes a mess that eventually stops working. I am conducting an experiment rigth now. There is potential, that eventually it will actually be able to do what engineers do. But not yet.

commenters.name: Aleksandr Ishchenko

commenters.commentText: Arguing at the coffee machine is often a sign of juniority. And when they understand your idea about the fact that code is not a holy grail but rather a business instrument, we call them "software developers". And when we can explain this idea to other developers, we call them "seniors."

commenters.headline: Staff Software Engineer, looking for new opportunities
commenters.commentText: I have to disagree with your bolded statement.  You named two criteria.1. It solves the problem now.2. It can be changed when requirements shift.  Presumably you mean "easily changed".I think you need to add:3. It is relatively easy to maintain.What makes code maintainable?  There are quite a few factors, but one of the big ones is having clear invariants: Things that are required to be true, and that the entirety of the code can rely on.  This makes it much easier to limit the possible edge cas

commenters.commentText: I read this and think hmm might be spending the latter half of my career refactoring/rewriting AI created spaghetti code tightly coupled hairballs. I would be terrified if I had business mission critical code only written, under

commenters.name: Matt E.
commenters.headline: Senior Software Engineer at Anthology
commenters.commentText: "Works on my machine" code works also. Should that now be acceptable in production too?  Why not just eliminate all coding standards just because something works?  The time spent writing proper code will be far lower than the time spent debugging, maintaining and ultimately refactoring poorly written code that introduces a lot of hidden nightmare scenarios. That's at the heart of the problem...you are asking to trade a small extra cost upfront to write good code with a large cost later of fixes,
